text: What Criston Cole's arc actually illustrates is how honor culture destroys people who can't maintain it. His turn from Rhaenyra's protector to her most vicious enemy follows the same logic as Jaime Lannister's arc in GoT but without the redemption — he doubles down on cruelty because acknowledging his failure would mean acknowledging he has none of the virtue he built his identity around.
label: analysis
notes: extended thematic reading with cross-series comparison - textbook analysis

text: He is one of the Realms most capable warriors. Crucially he is taken advantage of by a superior who uses him for sexual gratification. Cole clearly feels conflicted about the concept of his tarnished honor and in his eyes fails to reclaim it making him a bitter and violent man. Something most viewers seem to have missed is that the writers state his internal conflict as a literal fear of castration. Ultimately what seems to drive his character is the unconscious and correct assessment that the society he inhabits is royally screwed up.
label: analysis
notes: extended character analysis with specific textual evidence and psychological reading

text: Criston Cole is probably a good character concept because he's the only lowborn main character. He was a commoner trained to fight not to live among nobles. He wasn't raised for scheming and politicking so he is obviously lost. The person who chose him for that job also put him in constant mortal danger just because she was horny.
label: analysis
notes: character context argument grounded in social structure of the world

text: I feel like the necklace scene went over the heads of lots of people. The reason she didn't offer her own life was because she heard Blood tell Cheese that they would not get paid if they didn't get the head of a son. Given what she heard it would not make sense for her to offer her own life when it has zero value to these assassins who are here to get paid.

notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - close reading of scene logic to defend character choice

text: The big problem is that we don't spend enough time with Helena to understand her so her reactions as a neurodivergent person seem surprising because they are not typical. As if she's reliving the moment and trying her best not to play along but it almost seems like she's having an out-of-body experience disassociating from it. Had we gotten more character moments I think it would've solved this issue.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - identifies root cause of scene's weakness in character establishment

text: I thought Helaena's reaction was even more heartbreaking with her being neurodivergent because it adds that extra layer of vulnerability. Healthcare workers who regularly break bad news say that Helaena's reaction was actually very realistic for how some people react. I like that this isn't a standard portrayal of grief. It's more nuanced and disturbing in its own way.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - defends scene choice using real-world parallel and character consistency

text: This scene is indicative of a weird choice for the overall writing direction for the show. It seems like Condal is making too many things in the Dance come from miscommunication and happenstance. But that softens our feelings toward the purposeful actions of characters. GOT made us hate iconic characters because they revealed the underbelly of human nature - our own cruelty malice greed. We loved to hate characters like Joffrey because the choices that little prick made were very intentional and awful.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - identifies a systematic writing tendency and traces its effect on audience emotional response

text: The plan wasn't an accident but B&C stumbling upon the twins while searching for Aemond was as well as guessing which child to kill rather than cruelly forcing Helaena to choose then killing the other. Don't get me wrong the whole thing was evil but the intentionality of Daemon's plan and how it plays out in the books is another level of malice. It's a subtle difference but one that matters.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - book/show comparison focused on how intent vs accident changes moral weight

text: I believe Helaena stumbling upon Alicent and Criston after the murder of her son is to bring attention to Alicent. She had said at the beginning of the episode that they cannot do that again so she obviously knows it's wrong. The fact that she had the lord commander there with her instead of at his post could have been fundamental to preventing the murder of Jaehaerys. You can even see her change directions from one side of the bed where Alicent and Criston are to the other where she ultimately sits on the floor.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - reads a staging/blocking detail to argue for intentional thematic meaning in the Alicole scene

text: Having B&C in episode one was a very poor choice. S1 ended on an absolutely heartbreaking scene of a kid being murdered. Then S2 starts with another scene of another kid being murdered and it just lessens the impact. Ideally episode 1 would reintroduce us to the characters and linger more on Helaena. Show them dragon-proofing the city show all the efforts they make to protect the castle from war only for B&C to sneak in because the guards were too occupied protecting against dragons.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - structural critique about episode ordering and its effect on emotional impact

text: I was underwhelmed not because it didn't match the book but because it lacked real vision. First we are following Blood and Cheese throughout their entire break-in which removes dramatic tension. This could have worked if they played up the slapstick aspects of their characters and got us to like them somewhat - then there would be a dramatic tonal shift during the assassination. Instead we follow two bland assassins all the way into the bedroom with no issues at all.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - identifies specific directorial choice (POV following assassins) as source of tension failure

text: The assassination feels unrealistic. We're supposed to see B&C as idiots bumbling to the target but then we're supposed to think the royal family are idiots because they have no guards. The scene is a victim of boardroom style writing of modern TV - having to deal with arbitrary character decisions made by previous writers and trying to fit that into a story dictated by a book that the show has strayed from. In the books the scene has a purpose - to show the ugly face of the Blacks which fully instigates the war. Here that purpose is watered down.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - identifies internal consistency problem AND connects it to the scene's narrative function in both versions

text: I didn't hate it. Saban's Helaena has never been portrayed as the type of person to have big emotional displays. It was also heavily implied she experienced a vision of the event ahead of time with the I'm afraid of the rats line so the quiet horror and disassociation fits with that. People say it should have been on par with the Red Wedding but Helaena and her kids are side characters. They're hardly on the same level as Catelyn and Robb.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - defends scene by connecting character's established behavior to her reaction AND uses cross-show comparison to calibrate expectations

text: The book version of B&C is way better. One big issue is there was no suspense. They ask Helaena to tell them who the son is and she immediately does it with zero hesitation. She should have refused and pleaded for them to kill her instead. Then they should have threatened to kill them both unless she told them. After that she should have reluctantly told them. Helaena in the show comes across as not even trying to protect her child's life.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - scene-by-scene comparison identifying specific missing dramatic beats

text: They made too many changes at once. Individually some of those changes may have worked - Alicent outside with Cole only 2 kids being there Helaena being detached. Like 1 or 2 changes would have been fine. But the entire thing all at once was too much for such an anticipated scene. Those choices also fall apart because there are too many inconsistencies - they sneak in but the doors are wide open when they get there.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - argues that cumulative effect of changes is the problem even if individual changes might work - specific structural critique

text: What angers me the most is not the changes to the scene itself but the motive behind it. When deviating from the books they portray the Blacks as incapable of being responsible for such sheer horror while simultaneously making Alicent a complete hypocrite by showing her having sex with Criston thus stripping away any sympathy we had for her. In the books Daemon never sent the ratcatcher after Aemond. He specifically sent them to kill the little child - a cold-hearted act of vengeance that the show refused to show.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - argues the changes reveal a systematic bias in how the show portrays the two factions

text: For truly good writing getting the details spoiled shouldn't matter if it was executed well - and for me this time it did matter. Phia did a great job with what she was given and the sawing sounds were truly haunting. But almost every change works together to undermine the impact of the scene. Individually the changes are somewhat interesting but as a whole they appear to undermine each other.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - uses the test of whether spoilers ruin a scene as a measure of execution quality - sophisticated craft argument

text: In the show the assassins themselves changing their target from Aemond to Aegon's kids felt jarring because in the books Daemon never sent them after such a skilled warrior like Aemond. He specifically sent them to kill the little child. The show version makes it seem accidental rather than the cold deliberate cruelty that it was in the books. That deliberateness is what makes Daemon genuinely monstrous in this moment.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - book/show comparison centered on intentionality and its effect on character moral weight

text: Olivia Cooke worked so hard to make Alicent a multidimensional character. Having her having sex with Cole instead of witnessing her grandson's murder means we lose the moment that shapes Helaena and Alicent's characters for the rest of the story. Not only do fans feel no sympathy for a woman losing her grandson they are able to rationalize it was her fault since she was sleeping with a royal bodyguard. What should have been a defining moment for the character is at best a joke and at worst an excuse for Daemon.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - argues a specific casting/writing decision has downstream effects on audience sympathy and character arc

text: Blood and Cheese shouldn't be about what Alicent did wrong or what Criston did wrong. It should be about what Daemon did wrong. Its original narrative purpose is to establish the Blacks as not necessarily the good side of the dispute. The fact that the writers undercut a scene that should have been focused on Helaena's reaction to her young child being murdered to go wow Alicent and Criston sure are irresponsible hypocrites is wild.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - identifies the scene's original narrative function and argues the changes betray it

text: The Sophie's choice as adapted onto screen was not the same and didn't make sense. Cheese himself said they can look for a cock to identify the boy themselves. They only asked the mother to make it quick. So there was no element of Helaena having to actively choose which of her sons was murdered and then seeing the other one get killed - a choice that hugely lowered the emotional intensity.
label: analysis
notes: Blood and Cheese megathread - close reading of the scene's internal logic to explain why the adapted version of the choice doesn't work

text: I liked the B&C scene but I would have wished to still see Helaena beg for them to take her life instead and eventually have to choose between the twins.
